q: Why does this throw ConcurrentModificationException?

opts.t: You cannot remove from a List inside a for-each
ArrayList does not support remove()
opts.why: ArrayList.remove() exists; the problem is the iterator detects structural modification.

opts.t: equals() cannot be used for String comparison here
opts.why: equals() is correct for String; the iteration structure is the issue.

why: The enhanced for-each uses an Iterator internally. Calling list.remove() outside the iterator modifies the list's modCount, which the iterator detects on the next hasNext()/next() call and throws ConcurrentModificationException. Use iterator.remove() or removeIf() instead.
fj: "You can remove items during iteration if you're careful."
fs: "Use iterator.remove() or list.removeIf() — never mutate a collection inside for-each."

q: What does this print?

opts.t: true, false
true, true
opts.why: new String() always allocates a new object on the heap, so == is false.

opts.t: false, false
opts.why: s and t are string literals; the JVM interns them to the same reference, so s==t is true.

opts.t: false, true
opts.why: Literals are interned (same ref); new String() is a distinct object.

why: String literals are interned by the JVM — s and t point to the same object in the string pool, so == is true. new String("hello") forces a new heap object, so s==u is false. Always use equals() for value comparison.
fj: "== compares the string content for String variables."
fs: "Use equals() for strings; == tests reference identity and behaves differently for literals vs new String()."

q: This class overrides equals() but not hashCode(). What breaks?

opts.t: null — HashMap can't find the key
"A" — equals() is enough for HashMap lookup
opts.why: HashMap first compares hashCode(); without override, two distinct objects have different hash codes and land in different buckets.

opts.t: Throws NullPointerException
opts.why: No NPE here; the map simply returns null when the bucket lookup fails.

why: HashMap uses hashCode() to find the bucket, then equals() to confirm the key. Without overriding hashCode(), two logically equal Points hash to different buckets and get() returns null. Always override both equals() and hashCode() together.
fj: "equals() is all that matters for Map key lookups."
fs: "equals + hashCode are a contract — break one and collections misbehave."

q: Building a large String in a loop — which fix is best?

opts.t: Use StringBuilder and append()
Use String.format() inside the loop
opts.why: String.format() still creates a new String each iteration — same O(n²) allocation problem.

opts.t: Use String.concat() instead of +=
opts.why: concat() also creates a new String object each call, so performance is equivalent to +=.

opts.t: Use char[] and convert at the end
opts.why: That works but StringBuilder already does this more cleanly and is the standard idiom.

why: Each += on a String allocates a new object, giving O(n²) memory churn. StringBuilder maintains a resizable internal buffer and append() is O(1) amortised, reducing the total work to O(n).
fj: "String concatenation in a loop is fine — the JVM optimizes it."
fs: "Use StringBuilder.append() in loops; JVM only optimizes concatenation outside loops."

q: What happens at runtime?

opts.t: NullPointerException on the unboxing line
s is assigned 0 (default int)
opts.why: Java does not silently convert null to 0; unboxing null throws NPE.

opts.t: Compiles fine and prints null
opts.why: Primitive int cannot hold null — the unboxing Integer→int calls intValue() on null and throws.

why: scores.get("alice") returns the Integer null. Assigning it to a primitive int triggers auto-unboxing which calls null.intValue(), throwing NullPointerException. Use getOrDefault() or an explicit null check before unboxing.
fj: "Assigning null to an int just gives 0."
fs: "Unboxing a null Integer always throws NPE — guard with getOrDefault() or a null check."

q: What is wrong with this Optional usage?

opts.t: get() throws NoSuchElementException if empty
get() returns null when the Optional is empty
opts.why: Optional.get() never returns null; it throws NoSuchElementException on an empty Optional.

opts.t: Optional does not have a get() method
opts.why: Optional.get() exists but is considered unsafe; prefer orElse() or orElseGet().

why: Calling Optional.get() without first checking isPresent() (or using orElse/orElseGet/orElseThrow) throws NoSuchElementException when the Optional is empty. The safe pattern is name.orElse("default") or name.orElseThrow(() -> new RuntimeException("missing")).
fj: "Optional.get() is safe — it just returns null if empty."
fs: "Never call Optional.get() without a presence check; prefer orElse() or orElseThrow()."

q: This code leaks resources — pick the correct fix.

opts.t: Wrap in try-with-resources
Call r.close() in a finally block
opts.why: A finally block is better than nothing but is more verbose; try-with-resources is the idiomatic Java 7+ solution and also handles suppressed exceptions correctly.

opts.t: Use Files.readAllLines() instead
opts.why: readAllLines() closes the file, but that changes the API contract; the question asks how to fix resource handling, not to switch methods.

why: If readLine() throws, the current code skips r.close() and leaks the file descriptor. try-with-resources (Java 7+) guarantees the AutoCloseable is closed whether an exception occurs or not, and properly attaches suppressed exceptions to the primary exception.
fj: "Just call close() at the end — exceptions are rare."
fs: "Use try-with-resources for any AutoCloseable; it handles both normal and exceptional paths."

q: What type does Collectors.groupingBy return here?

opts.t: Map<Integer, List<String>>
Map<Integer, String>
opts.why: groupingBy downstream defaults to toList(), so each value is a List<String>, not a single String.

opts.t: Map<String, List<String>>
opts.why: The classifier is String::length which returns an Integer, so the key type is Integer, not String.

opts.t: List<Map.Entry<Integer,String>>
opts.why: groupingBy always returns a Map, not a List of entries.

why: Collectors.groupingBy(classifier) produces a Map where keys are the classifier's return type (Integer from String::length) and values are List<T> (the default downstream collector). The concrete runtime type is a HashMap.
fj: "groupingBy groups by string key, so the map key is String."
fs: "groupingBy key type matches the classifier's return type — here Integer (length), values are List<String>."

q: What is printed?

opts.why: 3.5 would require at least one operand to be a double or a cast; both a and b are int, so integer division truncates.

opts.t: Compile error — cannot assign int to double
opts.why: The assignment is valid; widening conversion applies. The issue is the division happens as int before the widening.

why: a / b performs integer division (both operands are int), yielding 3, which is then widened to 3.0 when stored in the double. To get 3.5 use (double) a / b or declare one operand as double.
fj: "The result variable is double so the division is done in double precision."
fs: "Cast before dividing: (double) a / b — the result type doesn't affect the operand types."

q: This loop prints fewer items than expected — why?

opts.t: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException on the last iteration
Only prints the first element
opts.why: The loop runs from 0 to arr.length (inclusive), printing all 5 elements then crashing on i=5.

opts.t: Skips the last element
opts.why: The condition i <= arr.length causes one extra iteration (i=5) which throws, not skips.

why: Valid indices are 0..arr.length-1. The condition i <= arr.length allows i to reach arr.length (5), causing arr[5] to throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. The fix is i < arr.length.
fj: "<= arr.length is safer to make sure you don't miss the last element."
fs: "Array bounds are 0..length-1; always use i < arr.length, never <=."

q: Will this singleton ever publish a partially-constructed object to another thread?

opts.t: Yes — inst is not volatile, so the first read can see a stale non-null value pointing to a partially-constructed object
No — the inner synchronized block guarantees visibility for all threads
opts.why: The outer read of inst happens outside the synchronized block without a memory barrier, so a thread may see a stale reference.

opts.t: No — the JVM always flushes writes when exiting a constructor
opts.why: The JVM does not guarantee constructor writes are visible to threads that read the reference outside a synchronization point.

why: The first null-check on inst is outside the synchronized block and reads a plain (non-volatile) field. Another thread may observe the stale reference of a partially-initialized object due to instruction reordering. Marking inst as volatile establishes a happens-before edge that fixes the double-checked locking pattern.
fj: "Double-checked locking is safe as long as the inner block is synchronized."
fs: "DCL requires volatile on the field so the reference write is visible before any read outside the lock."

q: What does this parallel stream operation print?

opts.t: Unpredictable — may print 0, 1, 2, or 2 with corrupted data
2 — parallelStream guarantees thread-safe collection writes
opts.why: parallelStream does not synchronize writes to the collector; ArrayList is not thread-safe.

opts.t: Throws ConcurrentModificationException
opts.why: CME is possible but not guaranteed; the more common failure is silent data corruption or a wrong size.

why: ArrayList is not thread-safe. Multiple threads calling add() concurrently can corrupt the internal array, resulting in lost elements or an incorrect size. Use a concurrent collector: .collect(Collectors.toList()) or Collectors.toConcurrentMap(), which handle thread safety internally.
fj: "parallelStream() handles the thread safety of the terminal operation automatically."
fs: "Never mutate a non-thread-safe collection from a parallel stream; use a thread-safe collector instead."

q: A web-app uses this ThreadLocal — what goes wrong under a thread pool?

opts.t: Pooled threads reuse CTX from a previous request if remove() is not called
ThreadLocal values are reset automatically at the end of each request
opts.why: The JVM does not automatically call remove() when a thread returns to a pool; the value persists until explicitly removed.

opts.t: ThreadLocal is not accessible from child threads, so the service layer always gets null
opts.why: ThreadLocal values are accessible within the same thread; InheritableThreadLocal is needed for child threads. The leak concern here is the same thread being reused.

why: Thread-pool threads are reused across requests. Without CTX.remove() in a finally block after each request, the next request served by the same thread inherits the previous UserCtx, causing privilege escalation or stale data bugs. Always call ThreadLocal.remove() in a finally block or filter cleanup.
fj: "ThreadLocal is per-thread so it resets between requests automatically."
fs: "Always remove() a ThreadLocal in a finally block when threads are pooled — stale values cross request boundaries."

q: What happens to the exception thrown inside thenApply()?

opts.t: The exception is silently swallowed — no log, no rethrow, no crash
The JVM logs an unhandled exception on the ForkJoinPool thread
opts.why: CompletableFuture wraps stage exceptions; they are not propagated to the thread's uncaught-exception handler unless the future is observed.

opts.t: thenApply() propagates the exception to the calling thread immediately
opts.why: thenApply() is asynchronous; exceptions are captured inside the future and only re-thrown when you call get() or join().

why: Exceptions thrown inside CompletableFuture stages are captured and stored in the future as a CompletionException. If no code ever calls get(), join(), or exceptionally() on the future, the exception is permanently silently discarded. Always chain .exceptionally() or call join() so failures are observable.
fj: "Exceptions in async stages will eventually surface on the thread that started them."
fs: "A CompletableFuture without a join/get or exceptionally handler is a silent exception black hole."

q: ConcurrentHashMap.compute() — what is the subtle contract violation here?

opts.t: Calling map.compute() or map.merge() inside compute() can deadlock if both keys hash to the same bin — compute() holds a bin-level lock and a nested write on the same bin will block forever
compute() is not atomic so the list may be modified by another thread mid-compute
opts.why: compute() IS atomic per key; the issue is that calling map operations on OTHER keys inside the remapping function can cause lock ordering deadlocks.

opts.t: ArrayList inside a ConcurrentHashMap value is unsafe; use CopyOnWriteArrayList
opts.why: Thread safety of the value object is a separate concern; the immediate deadlock risk from nested map ops is the critical bug here.

why: ConcurrentHashMap.compute() holds a bin-level lock for the duration of the remapping function. Calling another write operation (compute, merge, put) inside the remapping function can deadlock if both keys hash to the same bin. The remapping function must be short and must not invoke write operations on the same map. The remapping function must be fast and must not call back into the same map.
fj: "It's fine to read from the same map inside compute() — reads are non-blocking."
fs: "Never call back into a ConcurrentHashMap from inside its own compute/merge lambda — deadlock risk."

q: What happens-before guarantee is missing here?

opts.t: A thread calling consume() may see ready=true but value=0 due to reordering
The JVM always writes fields in source order, so value=42 is always visible before ready=true
opts.why: The JVM and CPU can reorder plain field writes in the absence of a memory barrier; source order does not imply execution order for other threads.

opts.t: There is no bug — boolean fields are inherently atomic and visible
opts.why: Boolean field reads/writes are individually atomic but visibility across threads requires a happens-before edge (volatile, synchronized, or lock).

why: Without volatile or synchronized, the JVM/CPU may reorder the stores: ready=true can become visible to another thread before value=42. Marking ready as volatile is sufficient (volatile write is ordered after all prior writes in the same thread), but the simplest fix is marking both fields volatile or using a lock.
fj: "Plain boolean flags are safe for signalling between threads."
fs: "Use volatile on the guard flag; volatile write establishes happens-before for all writes preceding it."

q: A shared-library singleton holds a mutable Map. What is the org-wide blast radius?

opts.t: Any caller can mutate the shared props map, corrupting config for all other callers in the same JVM
The singleton pattern itself is the flaw — use dependency injection instead
opts.why: DI is a valid architectural concern but not the immediate bug; the leaked mutable reference is the concrete org-wide blast-radius issue.

opts.t: HashMap is not thread-safe; replacing with ConcurrentHashMap fixes the flaw
opts.why: Thread safety helps but does not prevent a caller from intentionally or accidentally calling clear() or put() and corrupting the shared state for all callers.

why: getProps() returns the live internal HashMap. Any service can call Config.get().getProps().clear() or add fake keys, affecting every other service sharing the same classloader. The fix is to return Collections.unmodifiableMap(props) or a defensive copy, and never expose mutable state from a shared singleton.
fj: "The callers know not to mutate config — this is a documentation concern, not a code flaw."
fs: "A shared singleton must never leak a mutable reference — return unmodifiableMap() or a copy to contain blast radius."

q: A platform team ships this in a shared lib. What is the ABI-breaking flaw?

opts.t: Adding a default method is source-compatible but can cause a compile-time diamond-conflict in consumer code that implements two interfaces with the same default method signature, or IncompatibleClassChangeError at link time in pre-compiled consumers caught in such a diamond conflict
Interfaces cannot have default methods; this will not compile
opts.why: Java 8+ supports default methods in interfaces; this is syntactically valid.

opts.t: The change is fully backward-compatible; all existing consumers compile and run unchanged
opts.why: If a consumer has a class implementing both EventHandler and another interface with an incompatible onError signature, or a superclass with a conflicting method, the default method resolution can fail at link time.

why: Default methods are source-compatible additions, but if a downstream class implementing EventHandler already inherits or declares a method with the same name but an incompatible signature, the compiler will report a diamond-conflict error, or the JVM will throw IncompatibleClassChangeError at link time in pre-compiled consumers — at link/runtime, not at the library compile time. Platform teams must version interfaces carefully and document all default-method additions as potentially breaking for diamond-inheritance scenarios.
fj: "Adding a default method to an interface is always backward-compatible."
fs: "Default methods in shared interfaces can cause diamond-inheritance conflicts in consumers — treat them as potentially breaking changes."

q: Dozens of services share this ExecutorService factory. What is the systemic risk?

opts.t: newCachedThreadPool() is unbounded — a traffic spike creates unlimited threads, exhausting OS resources for all services in the JVM
A static ExecutorService is garbage-collected when unused, shutting down in-flight tasks
opts.why: A static final field is a GC root and will never be collected while the class is loaded.

opts.t: The risk is thread starvation — a fixed pool blocks callers when all threads are busy
opts.why: Thread starvation is a risk of fixed pools, not cached pools. The cached pool creates new threads on demand, which is the unbounded growth risk.

why: newCachedThreadPool() creates a new thread for every submitted task that has no idle thread available, with no upper bound. Under a request burst across 40 services sharing this pool, the JVM can spawn tens of thousands of threads, exhausting OS thread limits and crashing all services sharing the JVM. The fix is Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n) or a ThreadPoolExecutor with a bounded queue and a RejectedExecutionHandler that sheds load gracefully.
fj: "Cached thread pools are efficient because they reuse threads; they scale automatically."
fs: "Always bound shared executors; newCachedThreadPool() in a shared library is an unbounded resource bomb."

q: This retry util is deployed across 60 microservices. What systemic failure does it cause?

opts.t: Synchronous retry with no backoff or jitter triggers a retry storm that amplifies load on a struggling downstream by 60x
The unchecked RuntimeException wrapping breaks callers that handle specific checked exceptions
opts.why: Exception wrapping is a valid concern but has local blast radius; the retry storm is the org-wide systemic risk.

opts.t: The method is not thread-safe and will corrupt shared state under concurrent calls
opts.why: The method has no shared mutable state; each invocation is independent. Concurrency is not the issue here.

why: When a downstream service degrades, all 60 callers immediately retry up to `times` times with zero delay. This multiplies the request rate by up to 60*times simultaneously — a classic retry storm. The degraded service, already overloaded, gets crushed and cannot recover. The fix is exponential backoff + jitter (e.g. 100ms * 2^attempt + random(0..100ms)), a circuit breaker, and ideally a shared rate limiter.
fj: "Retrying quickly is better — it reduces the total time to success for the caller."
fs: "Shared retry logic without backoff + jitter is a force-multiplier on downstream failures — always add both."
